title: practical english usage - Part 232
content:
(e.g.          which  means ‘ big fire', and is used in headlines to refer to any fire).

are chosen not because they are short, but because they sound dramatic bid, not often have in ordinary language (e.g.       meaning 'attempt'). Other words 'restrict' or 'restriction'), and some are used in special senses which  they do curb, short words in headlines are unusual in ordinary language (e.g.         meaning Short words save space, and so they are very common in headlines. Some of the 3 vocabulary For other styles with special grammar, ►    291 .

CRISIS   OVER   BY  SEPTEMBER?

A  question mark (?) is often used when something is not certain.

CRASH   DRIVER   'HAD BEEN  DRINKING' else, and that the report does not necessarily claim that they are true.

Quotation marks ('...') are used to show that words were said by somebody STRIKES:   PM  TO  ACT  MOTORWAY       CRASH:   DEATH   TOLL   RISES about it.

A  colon (:) is often used to separate the subject of a headline from what is said i FOOTBALL    MANAGER   IN CAR  CRASH hospital  BOSS   axedaspatientsdie ... because patients have died.) (= in As and     are often used instead of longer connecting expressions.

(= A  boy has found a safe.) -  BOY   FOUND   SAFE (= The  missing boy has been found safe; he is safe.) her critics.) AID PROTEST:    PRESIDENT    ATTACKS    CRITICS (= . . . the President has attacked - AID PROTEST:    PRESIDENT    ATTACKED (= ... the President has been attacked.) meanings, not past tenses (which are rare in headlines). Compare: HELD,  ATTACKED Note that forms like                         are usually past participles with passive SIX  KILLED  IN EXPLOSION (= Six people have been killed . . .) MURDER    HUNT:   MAN  HELD (= ... a man is being held by police.) Auxiliary verbs are usually dropped from passive structures.
